m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main function of the Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler in the .NET Framework?

Options.OptionText: To convert IL code into native machine code at runtime
To optimize the performance of .NET applications
To interpret IL code at runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: The Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler is a component of the .NET Framework that is responsible for converting Intermediate Language (IL) code into native machine code at runtime. This allows .NET applications to run faster, as the IL code does not have to be interpreted and can be directly executed by the processor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following JIT compilers compiles methods only when they are called?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Normal-JIT compiler in .NET compiles methods only when they are called for the first time. This helps in optimizing the performance by avoiding the compilation of methods that are never used.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the .NET Framework Class Library (FCL)?

Options.OptionText: A collection of classes, interfaces, and value types that are included in the .NET Framework
A set of tools for building .NET applications

m_Item1.Explanation: The .NET Framework Class Library (FCL) is a comprehensive collection of reusable classes, interfaces, and value types that are included in the .NET Framework. It provides a wide range of functionality for developing applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is .NET Core primarily used for?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core is a cross-platform framework primarily used for developing applications that can run on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In Visual Studio,
________ pops up a list of methods that can be called on that object,instead of typing the full method
name.
m_Item1.Explanation: IntelliSense is a code-completion aid that includes a number of features: List Members, Parameter Info, Quick Info, and Complete Word. These features help you to learn more about the code you're using, keep track of the parameters you're typing, and add calls to properties and methods with only a few keystrokes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a key feature of .NET Core compared to the traditional .NET Framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core is open-source and cross-platform, allowing developers to build and run applications on different operating systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
The .NET Framework is designed for cross-language compatibility, which means, simply, that .NET
components can interact with each other no matter what supported language they were written in
originally.

Options.OptionText: This level of cross-language compatibility is possible because of the common language runtime.
This level of cross-language compatibility is possible because of the common Type System
This level of cross-language compatibility is possible because of the Common Language Specification

m_Item1.Explanation: The Common Language Specification (CLS) defines a set of rules and guidelines that language compilers must follow to ensure interoperability between different .NET languages. This includes guidelines for data types, naming conventions, and other language features that enable components written in different languages to interact seamlessly within the .NET Framework.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the JIT compiler in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To convert high-level code into intermediate code
To execute code directly without compilation
To compile intermediate language (IL) code into machine code at runtime
To interpret IL code without compilation

m_Item1.Explanation: The primary purpose of the JIT compiler in .NET is to convert the intermediate language (IL) code into machine code at runtime. This allows the application to be executed on the specific hardware and operating system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the term "Just-In-Time" in JIT compilation refer to?

Options.OptionText: Compiling code at the moment it is needed

m_Item1.Explanation: "Just-In-Time" in JIT compilation means that the code is compiled at the moment it is needed, i.e., at runtime. This approach ensures that only the necessary parts of the code are compiled, which can help in improving the startup time and overall performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about Managed Code (MC)?

Options.OptionText: Managed code(MC) is compiled by the JIT(Just In Time) compilers
Managed code(MC) where resources are Garbage Collected(GC)
Managed code (MC) written to target the services of the Common Language Runtime (CLR).

m_Item1.Explanation:
Managed code (MC) is compiled by the JIT (Just In Time) compilers: Managed code is compiled to an intermediate language (IL) which is then compiled to native code by the JIT compiler at runtime.

Managed code (MC) where resources are Garbage Collected (GC): Managed code benefits from the automatic memory management provided by the CLR, which includes garbage collection.

Managed code (MC) written to target the services of the Common Language Runtime (CLR): Managed code is specifically written to run under the management of the CLR, leveraging its features like security, memory management, and exception handling.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is strong naming in .NET?

Options.OptionText: A way to encrypt an assembly
A way to version an assembly
A way to give an assembly a unique identity
A way to sign an assembly with a digital certificate

m_Item1.Explanation: Strong naming is the process of giving an assembly a unique identity by using a public key and a digital signature. It ensures that the assembly is unique and can be verified by the runtime, preventing name collisions and ensuring that the assembly has not been tampered with. Strong naming is required for shared assemblies that are deployed in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements are correct about JIT?

Options.OptionText: JIT compiler compiles instructions into machine code at run time.
The code compiler by the JIT compiler runs under CLR.
The instructions compiled by JIT compilers are written in native code.

m_Item1.Explanation:
JIT compiler compiles instructions into machine code at run time: This is the primary function of the JIT compiler. It converts the Intermediate Language (IL) code into machine code just before execution.

The code compiled by the JIT compiler runs under CLR: The machine code generated by the JIT compiler is executed within the environment provided by the Common Language Runtime (CLR), which handles various runtime services such as memory management, security, and exception handling.

The instructions compiled by JIT compilers are written in native code: After JIT compilation, the IL code is converted to native machine code specific to the operating system and hardware architecture it is running on.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the root of the .NET type hierarchy?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The root of the .NET type hierarchy is:
System.Object
In the .NET framework, all types, whether they are value types or reference types, ultimately derive from System.Object. This means that every type inherits the methods defined in the System.Object class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Code that targets the Common Language Runtime is known as
m_Item1.Explanation:
Code that targets the Common Language Runtime (CLR) is known as:
Managed Code
Managed code is executed by the CLR, which provides various services such as garbage collection, type safety, exception handling, and security.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements correctly define .NET Framework?

Options.OptionText:
It is an environment for developing, building, deploying and executing Desktop Applications, Web
Applications and Web Services.
It is an environment for developing, building, deploying and executing only Web Applications.
It is an environment for developing, building, deploying and executing Distributed Applications.
It is an environment for developing, building, deploying and executing Web Services.

m_Item1.Explanation: The .NET Framework provides a comprehensive and consistent programming model for building applications that have visually stunning user experiences, seamless and secure communication, and the ability to model a range of business processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many types of JIT compilers available under CLR?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Just-in-time (JIT) compilers have three main types: pre-JIT, normal JIT, and Econo JIT:
  • Pre-JIT
    Compiles the entire MSIL code into native code in a single compilation cycle at the time of application deployment. The output file can only run on one compatible platform.
  • Normal JIT
    Compiles only the methods that are called at runtime, storing the compiled code in a memory cache called JITTED. When the same methods are called again, the compiled code from the cache is used for execution.
  • Econo JIT
    Compiles only the methods that are called at runtime, but removes them from memory after execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between a private assembly and a shared assembly in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Private assemblies can be used by multiple applications, while shared assemblies can only be used by one application
Private assemblies are stored in the GAC, while shared assemblies are not
Private assemblies are deployed with the application, while shared assemblies are deployed separately
Private assemblies are not versioned, while shared assemblies are versioned

m_Item1.Explanation: Private assemblies are deployed with the application that uses them and are typically stored in the application's directory. Shared assemblies, on the other hand, are deployed separately and are typically stored in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC). Private assemblies can only be used by the application that deploys them, while shared assemblies can be used by multiple applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is a GAC assembly typically stored on a Windows machine?
m_Item1.Explanation: GAC assembly typically stored on a Windows machine in  C:\\Windows\\Assembly  folder.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a feature of .NET Core compared to the traditional .NET Framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core does not have built-in support for WPF (Windows Presentation Foundation) applications, unlike the traditional .NET Framework which includes WPF as part of its feature set.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following namespaces is used for file I/O operations in the FCL?
m_Item1.Explanation: The System.IO namespace contains classes for handling input and output operations, such as reading from and writing to files and data streams, and interacting with the file system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which namespace in the FCL provides classes for working with collections, such as lists, queues, stacks, and dictionaries?
m_Item1.Explanation: The System.Collections namespace provides classes and interfaces for defining various collection types, such as lists, queues, stacks, and dictionaries. It includes both generic and non-generic collections.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Strong Name includes the

Options.OptionText: culture identity, and a public key token

m_Item1.Explanation:
A Strong Name in .NET includes:
- The name of the .NET assembly
- The version number
- The culture identity
- The public key token

A strong name uniquely identifies an assembly and ensures its uniqueness and integrity, allowing it to be referenced securely in other assemblies and in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tool can be used for creating and managing migrations in Entity Framework Core?
m_Item1.Explanation: Entity Framework Core migrations can be managed using the EF Package Manager Console, which provides commands for adding, applying, and reverting migrations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using Dependency Injection (DI) in .NET Core applications?

Options.OptionText: Simplifies unit testing and improves code maintainability
Reduces the size of the application binaries
Provides built-in support for microservices architecture
Increases the security of web applications

m_Item1.Explanation: Dependency Injection (DI) in .NET Core applications simplifies unit testing by facilitating loose coupling and improves code maintainability by promoting modular and reusable components.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
.entrypoint
 .maxstack 3
 .locals ([0] int32 ValueOne,
 [1] int32 ValueTwo,
 [2] int32 V_2,
 [3] int32 V_3)
 IL_0000: ldc.i4.s 10
 IL_0002: stloc.0
 IL_0003: ldc.i4.s 20
 IL_0005: stl
m_Item1.Explanation:
This is snippet of MSIL (Microsoft Intermediate Language) code
.entrypoint: Indicates the entry point of the application. This is where execution begins.
.maxstack 3: Specifies the maximum number of items on the operand stack during the execution of the method.
.locals ([0] int32 ValueOne, [1] int32 ValueTwo, [2] int32 V_2, [3] int32 V_3): Declares local variables within the method, specifying their types and index positions on the stack.
IL_0000, IL_0002, IL_0003, IL_0005: These are labels for individual instructions within the method, indicating their position in the IL code.
ldc.i4.s 10, ldc.i4.s 20: Load constant instructions that push the specified integer values (10 and 20) onto the stack.
stloc.0, stloc.1: Store local variable instructions that pop the top value from the stack and store it in the local variables at index 0 (ValueOne) and index 1 (ValueTwo), respectively.

m_Item1.QuestionText: From which one of the following locations does the garbage collector remove objects?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The garbage collector in .NET removes objects from the managed heap.
Managed Heap: This is where all .NET objects are allocated. The garbage collector periodically checks this heap to reclaim memory used by objects that are no longer in use.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about ASP.NET Core?

Options.OptionText: It is only compatible with IIS (Internet Information Services)
It supports both MVC and Razor Pages
It does not support dependency injection

m_Item1.Explanation: ASP.NET Core supports both MVC (Model-View-Controller) and Razor Pages, providing flexibility in web application development.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
John wants to look at a human readable representation of the metadata and intermediate language (IL)
code contained in a .NET Portable Executable (PE) file. Given the above scenario, what tool from the .NET
SDK should John use?
m_Item1.Explanation:
John should use the ildasm tool from the .NET SDK.
ildasm (IL Disassembler) is a tool provided by the .NET SDK that allows users to view the metadata and IL code contained within a .NET Portable Executable (PE) file. It provides a human-readable representation of the assembly's contents, including classes, methods, properties, and the IL instructions.
To use ildasm, John can run it from the command line, passing the path to the PE file as an argument. This tool is particularly useful for inspecting the internals of .NET assemblies during development or debugging.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the relationship between Common Type System (CTS) and Common Language Specification (CLS)?

Options.OptionText: NET Languages each offer a subset of the CTS and a superset of the CLS.
NET Languages each offer a superset of the CTS and a subset of the CLS.
NET Languages each offer either the CTS set or the CLS set.
NET Languages all offer the same superset of the CTS.

m_Item1.Explanation:
The relationship between the Common Type System (CTS) and the Common Language Specification (CLS) in .NET is as follows:
.NET Languages each offer a subset of the CTS and a superset of the CLS.

Here’s what this means:

  • Common Type System (CTS): Defines the types that can be used in the .NET framework. It includes basic types (like int, float, etc.), reference types, and user-defined types.

  • Common Language Specification (CLS): Defines a subset of rules that all .NET languages must adhere to in order to ensure interoperability. It specifies the minimum set of features and rules that all languages should support.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is the Class Loader located?

Options.OptionText: In the Common Language Runtime's (CLR) Virtual Execution Engine

m_Item1.Explanation:
The Class Loader in the Common Language Runtime (CLR) is located:
In the Common Language Runtime's (CLR) Virtual Execution Engine.
The Class Loader is responsible for loading types and their associated metadata from assemblies into memory when they are first accessed by the application. This process is integral to how the CLR manages types and their usage during runtime execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which hosting model is recommended for deploying .NET Core applications to cloud platforms like Azure?
m_Item1.Explanation: Out-of-process hosting model is recommended for deploying .NET Core applications to cloud platforms like Azure because it provides better scalability and isolation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which file is used to configure .NET Core applications for different environments?
m_Item1.Explanation: Configuration settings for .NET Core applications are typically stored in the `appsettings.json` file, which allows developers to define settings for different environments (development, staging, production, etc.).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following statements is true about MSIL code?

Options.OptionText: It is compiled to native code by JIT compilers.
It is only stored in assembly resource files.

m_Item1.Explanation:
The correct statement about MSIL (Microsoft Intermediate Language) code is:
It is architecture-specific.
 MSIL is an intermediate language that is architecture-independent. It is not tied to any specific processor or operating system architecture, which allows .NET applications to be portable across different platforms. When executed, MSIL is compiled by the JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler into native code specific to the underlying hardware architecture and operating system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tool is used to manage dependencies and package libraries in .NET Core projects?
m_Item1.Explanation: NuGet Package Manager is used to manage dependencies and package libraries in .NET Core projects by enabling easy installation, updating, and removal of packages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What Is CLS?
m_Item1.Explanation: CLS stands for Common Language Specification and it is a subset of CTS. It defines a set of rules and restrictions that every language must follow.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What Is DLL?
m_Item1.Explanation: Dynamic Link Library (DLL) is Microsoft's implementation of the shared library concept. A DLL file contains code and data that can be used by multiple programs at the same time, hence it promotes code reuse and modularization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What Is GAC?
m_Item1.Explanation: Each computer where the Common Language Runtime is installed has a machine-wide  code cache called the Global Assembly Cache. The Global Assembly Cache stores  assemblies specifically designated to be shared by several applications on the computer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following command-line entries would allow you to install an assembly into the GAC?
m_Item1.Explanation: You can use the .NET Global Assembly Cache utility (gacutil.exe) to add assemblies to the global assembly cache and to view the contents of the global assembly cache.

m_Item1.QuestionText: All Dot Net Compatible languages are Type safe. Which property makes it type safe?
m_Item1.Explanation:
All .NET compatible languages are type safe primarily due to the CTS (Common Type System). The CTS defines the rules for declaring, using, and managing types in the .NET runtime, ensuring that all types are strictly defined and consistently enforced across different languages.
Therefore, the property that makes .NET languages type safe is: CTS (Common Type System).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following depicts a Satellite Assembly?

Options.OptionText: Assembly used to localize the application.
Assembly created by Microsoft for .NET base Class Library.
Assembly created to share through satellite.

m_Item1.Explanation: A satellite assembly is a compiled library (DLL) that contains “localizable” resources specific to a given culture such as strings, bitmaps, etc.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the AssemblyVersion attribute in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To specify the version of the .NET Framework that the assembly targets
To specify the version of the assembly.
To specify the culture of the assembly
To specify the public key token of the assembly

m_Item1.Explanation: The AssemblyVersion attribute is used to specify the version of an assembly in .NET. It is a four-part number that consists of the major version, minor version, build number, and revision number. The version number is used by the runtime to ensure that the correct version of an assembly is loaded and to enable side-by-side execution of different versions of an assembly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a Shared Assembly in .NET?

Options.OptionText: An assembly that can only be used by one application
An assembly that can be used by multiple applications
An assembly that cannot be shared between applications
An assembly that is used only for testing purposes

m_Item1.Explanation: A shared assembly in .NET is a library of code that can be used by multiple applications. It is typically stored in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC) and can be accessed by any application that has the necessary permissions. Shared assemblies are useful for creating reusable components that can be shared across multiple applications, reducing development time and improving code maintainability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The heart of .NET framework is
m_Item1.Explanation:
The heart of the .NET Framework is the CLR (Common Language Runtime).

The CLR provides the execution environment for .NET programs, handling memory management, security, exception handling, and more.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The common language specification

Options.OptionText: describes the way components authored for the common language
provides a standardized way for events to be triggered
provides a standardized way for UI to be constructed
provides a standardized way for database handling

m_Item1.Explanation:
The Common Language Specification (CLS) describes a set of base rules and standards that ensure interoperability between components authored in different programming languages within the .NET framework. Specifically, it.
The CLS defines how components and libraries written in different .NET languages can interoperate seamlessly. By adhering to the CLS, developers can create reusable libraries that can be used across various .NET-compliant languages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: You want to examine and change the value of a variable in your C# application. You are developing using Visual Studio .NET. What window will allow you to change the value during execution?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Watch Window: For a focused view of specific variables, use the Watch window (Debug > Windows > Watch).
Immediate Window: To quickly evaluate expressions and change variables, use the Immediate window (Debug > Windows > Immediate).
Autos Window: To view variables involved in the current line of code, use the Autos window (Debug > Windows > Autos).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which line must exist in the AssemblyInfo.cs file in order to “sign” the assembly?
m_Item1.Explanation: To sign an assembly in .NET, you need to specify the key file that contains the strong name key. This is done using the [assembly: AssemblyKeyFile("myKeys.snk")] attribute.

m_Item1.QuestionText: To create a class file that can be added to a library, you would select which project type?
m_Item1.Explanation: Class Library  is used to create classes in .net.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The … namespace is the root of all namespaces containing all other namespaces as subordinates.
m_Item1.Explanation: In .NET, the System namespace is the root of all namespaces containing all other namespaces as subordinates. It contains basic types and common types and functions used throughout the .NET Framework.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can be used to debug .NET application?
m_Item1.Explanation:
To debug a .NET application, you can use:

  1. System.Diagnostics classes: These classes provide tools for debugging and tracing the execution of your applications. For example, you can use Debug and Trace classes for logging information.

  2. Runtime Debugger: The .NET runtime includes a just-in-time (JIT) debugger that can help diagnose issues in your applications when they occur.

  3. Visual Studio .NET: This integrated development environment (IDE) provides extensive debugging tools, including breakpoints, watch windows, call stacks, and more, making it one of the most powerful and commonly used tools for debugging .NET applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the Common Language Runtime (CLR) in the .NET Framework?

Options.OptionText: To compile code into machine language
To provide runtime services such as memory management

m_Item1.Explanation: The Common Language Runtime (CLR) in the .NET Framework provides runtime services such as memory management, including garbage collection, exception handling, and resource management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which assembly type contains executable application code?
m_Item1.Explanation: A Private Assembly is used by a single application and contains the application's executable code along with its dependencies.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What file extension is used for a .NET Assembly?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Assemblies are often contained in files with the extension .dll, which stands for Dynamic Link Library.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In .NET, what does JIT stand for?
m_Item1.Explanation: JIT stands for Just In Time, and it refers to the compilation process where MSIL code is converted into native machine code right before execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which component of the .NET framework is responsible for providing user interface services?
m_Item1.Explanation: Windows Forms is a component of the .NET framework used to develop rich user interfaces for Windows applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a .NET language?
m_Item1.Explanation: Python is not a native .NET language, although it can be used in the .NET environment through interoperability libraries.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the Global Assembly Cache (GAC) in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To store assemblies intended for shared use among applications
To compile automatic resources at runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: The Global Assembly Cache (GAC) is used to store assemblies that are intended for shared use by multiple applications on the machine.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the use of the 'using' statement in C#?

Options.OptionText: To specify code to be run at runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'using' statement in C# is used to define a scope at the end of which an object will be automatically disposed, helping to manage resources efficiently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: .NET Framework 3.5 includes which language features?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Framework 3.5 introduced language features such as lambda expressions and Language Integrated Query (LINQ) to enable developer productivity and more profound language integration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which .NET component provides a consistent programming model for building applications that have visually oriented user experiences?
m_Item1.Explanation: Windows Presentation Foundation (WPF) is the .NET component that provides a unified programming model for building applications with visually rich user interfaces.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of Intermediate Language (IL) in the .NET framework?

Options.OptionText: To increase the execution speed of applications
To provide a low-level language for operating system interactions
To serve as a platform-independent, verifiable code format
To allow direct communication between .NET languages and hardware

m_Item1.Explanation: Intermediate Language (IL), also known as Microsoft Intermediate Language (MSIL), is a CPU-independent and platform-independent language that allows .NET applications to be executed on any system with a compatible runtime. IL is later compiled to native code by the Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is TRUE about IL code in .NET?

Options.OptionText: IL code is executed directly by the operating system.
IL code is compiled to native code by a JIT compiler at runtime.
IL code can be written directly by developers instead of using high-level languages.
IL guarantees immediate execution of all stored coded routines.

m_Item1.Explanation: IL code is not directly executed by the operating system. Instead, it is compiled to native code specific to the underlying architecture by the Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler when the application runs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tool is used to disassemble a .NET assembly to examine the Intermediate Language (IL) code?
m_Item1.Explanation: ILDASM (Intermediate Language Disassembler) is a tool provided by .NET SDK that allows developers to view the MSIL code and other metadata of a .NET assembly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of IL, what does Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation refer to?

Options.OptionText: Compiling code from IL to native code before deployment
Interpreting the IL code line-by-line at runtime
Compiling IL to native code on-the-fly as methods are invoked
Translating IL to native code during the code writing phase

m_Item1.Explanation: Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation refers to compiling the IL code to native machine code on-the-fly, or just-in-time, as each method is called. This allows for optimized performance while maintaining platform independence.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the file extension commonly used for a .NET assembly containing IL code?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET assemblies are commonly distributed as DLL (Dynamic Link Library) files, which contain IL code and metadata needed for execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of an assembly in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To provide a user interface for applications
To define security roles and permissions
To define reusable modules within .NET applications

m_Item1.Explanation: An assembly in .NET is a compiled code library used for deployment, versioning, and security. It typically contains code and resources for a specific functionality, making it a reusable module.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which file extension is commonly associated with .NET assemblies?
m_Item1.Explanation: Assemblies in .NET commonly have the .dll (Dynamic Link Library) extension when they are used as libraries that can be shared or reused across different applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What tool is used to view the contents of an assembly?
m_Item1.Explanation: ILDASM (Intermediate Language Disassembler) is a tool provided by .NET Framework to view and analyze the contents of a .NET assembly in Intermediate Language (IL) code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of a strong name in a .NET assembly?

Options.OptionText: To encrypt the assembly contents for security
To ensure version compatibility with the CLR
To uniquely identify the assembly and ensure its authenticity

m_Item1.Explanation: A strong name adds a public key and a digital signature to an assembly, ensuring that the assembly has not been tampered with and is uniquely identified across the .NET framework.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a type of .NET assembly?
m_Item1.Explanation: In .NET, assemblies are typically categorized as either private or public (shared via the Global Assembly Cache), or dynamic when generated at runtime. 'Static assembly' is not a recognized category.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What component of an assembly contains metadata descriptions?
m_Item1.Explanation: The assembly manifest contains metadata that describes the assembly's version, culture, and public key, along with a list of all files that make up the assembly and referenced assemblies.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used to load an assembly at runtime in .NET?
m_Item1.Explanation: Assembly.Load is used to dynamically load an assembly into the current application domain at runtime, enabling the execution of its code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is stored in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC)?

Options.OptionText: Shared assemblies to be used by multiple applications

m_Item1.Explanation: The Global Assembly Cache (GAC) is used to store assemblies that are intended to be shared across multiple applications, providing a centralized location for version control and security management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What feature of assemblies ensures that components with the same name do not interfere in the GAC?
m_Item1.Explanation: Strong naming of assemblies, which includes the version number, culture, and a public key, guarantees that components with the same name do not conflict in the Global Assembly Cache.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which attribute can be applied to make an assembly COM-visible?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ComVisible attribute in an assembly's metadata determines whether types in the assembly are visible to COM (Component Object Model) components.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary difference between a .exe file and a .dll file in .NET?

Options.OptionText: exe file is a library while a .dll file is an application.
exe file is an executable file while a .dll file is a dynamically linked library.
dll file can run without a .exe file.
dll file is an executable file similar to a .exe file.

m_Item1.Explanation: A .exe file is an executable program, while a .dll file contains code and resources used by other programs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true regarding dependencies in .NET?

Options.OptionText: dll file can directly reference and execute a .exe file.
.exe files can be used for code reuse similar to .dll files.
exe file cannot depend on any .dll files.
dll file cannot execute independently without a .exe file or other program.

m_Item1.Explanation: A .dll file is a library that cannot run on its own; it needs to be called by an executable or another library.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a C# .NET solution, which file usually comprises the main entry point for the application?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .exe file in a .NET application contains the main method, serving as the entry point for the program execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the Common Language Runtime (CLR) in .NET work with .dll and .exe files?

Options.OptionText: CLR compiles both .exe and .dll files into machine code for execution.
CLR ignores all .dll files and executes only .exe files.
CLR compiles only .exe files; .dll files are executed as is.

m_Item1.Explanation: The CLR compiles both .exe and .dll files into machine code via Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation for execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What command is used to register a .NET assembly as a COM component?
m_Item1.Explanation: The regasm utility is used to register .NET assemblies for use as COM components.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary role of the Common Language Runtime (CLR) in .NET?
m_Item1.Explanation: The CLR is responsible for managing the execution of .NET programs, which includes executing managed code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a function of the CLR?
m_Item1.Explanation: The CLR provides automatic memory management, code access security, and exception handling but not file system management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which mechanism does the CLR provide to handle different types of errors during program execution?
m_Item1.Explanation: Exception handling is a mechanism provided by the CLR to handle runtime errors, ensuring robust and fault-tolerant execution of applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation in the CLR?

Options.OptionText: Convert source code to byte code
Convert IL code to machine code
Convert byte code to source code

m_Item1.Explanation: JIT Compilation converts Intermediate Language (IL) code to native machine code just before execution, improving runtime performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which component of the CLR helps in improving memory utilization during program execution?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Garbage Collector in the CLR deallocates and reclaims memory that is no longer in use, optimizing memory utilization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does CLR ensure type safety in .NET applications?

Options.OptionText: By verifying type compatibility at runtime
By converting all types to strings

m_Item1.Explanation: The CLR ensures type safety by verifying type compatibility at runtime, preventing type errors in .NET applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the significance of assemblies in the CLR?

Options.OptionText: They are used to store static resources
They are the building blocks of .NET applications

m_Item1.Explanation: Assemblies are the building blocks of .NET applications; they contain compiled code and metadata used by the CLR.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following tasks is managed by the CLR to enhance application security?
m_Item1.Explanation: The CLR manages code access security to ensure that code executes within the permissions granted to the user, enhancing application security.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does JIT stand for in the context of .NET technologies?
m_Item1.Explanation: JIT stands for Just-In-Time, which is a compilation process in .NET where the code is compiled at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following JIT compilation methods compiles the code into CPU-specific instructions immediately before it is executed?
m_Item1.Explanation: Just-In-Time Compilation compiles the intermediate language (IL) code to native code as it is needed at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of JIT compilation compiles methods on an as-needed basis?
m_Item1.Explanation: Normal JIT compilation compiles methods just before they are needed for execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In .NET, what is the primary role of the JIT compiler?

Options.OptionText: Compile managed code to native code

m_Item1.Explanation: The JIT compiler in .NET is responsible for converting the managed IL code into native code understood by the CPU.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following improves application startup performance by avoiding the overhead of JIT compilation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Ngen.exe pre-compiles IL to native code at installation time, thus improving startup performance by eliminating the JIT compilation overhead.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the advantage of using JIT compilation in .NET applications?

Options.OptionText: Compilation tailored to specific CPU architectures

m_Item1.Explanation: JIT compilation allows the runtime to optimize the code for the specific CPU architecture, improving performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: During execution of a .NET application, when does the JIT compiler typically perform its task?

Options.OptionText: After the application has finished executing
Just before each method is called

m_Item1.Explanation: The JIT compiler compiles each method just before it is first called, which allows for optimizations based on the current execution environment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which optimization technique is used by the JIT compiler to enhance performance in frequently executed paths of code?
m_Item1.Explanation: Code inlining replaces a method call site with the body of the method, reducing the overhead of the call and potentially improving performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C#, which of the following is responsible for automatic memory management?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Garbage Collector (GC) is responsible for automatic memory management in .NET by freeing up memory occupied by objects that are no longer in use.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is called by the Garbage Collector in C# before reclaiming the memory used by an object?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'Finalize' method is called by the Garbage Collector to perform cleanup operations before reclaiming an object's memory. In C#, it is implemented via a destructor syntax.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of implementing the IDisposable interface in C#?

Options.OptionText: To optimize memory allocation in managed heap

m_Item1.Explanation: The IDisposable interface is implemented in C# to provide a standardized method for releasing unmanaged resources, typically via the 'Dispose' method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of C# memory management, what is the main difference between a managed and unmanaged resource?

Options.OptionText: Managed resources are automatically deallocated; unmanaged resources are not.
Unmanaged resources are automatically deallocated; managed resources are not.
Both managed and unmanaged resources are deallocated automatically.
Neither managed nor unmanaged resources need deallocation.

m_Item1.Explanation: Managed resources are handled by the .NET runtime and their memory is automatically deallocated by the garbage collector. Unmanaged resources must be manually released by the developer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is a technique to optimize memory usage in .NET applications?
m_Item1.Explanation: Lazy initialization delays the creation of an object, the calculation of a value, or some other expensive processes until the first time it is needed, improving application performance by avoiding unnecessary memory allocation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of the Garbage Collector in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To manage memory by allocating objects on the heap.
To schedule the execution of threads efficiently.
To automatically manage the allocation and release of memory for applications.
To facilitate data binding in web applications.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Garbage Collector (GC) is responsible for automatically managing memory, freeing up space and ensuring efficient use by releasing memory that is no longer in use by the application.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following generations does NOT exist in .NET’s Garbage Collector?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET’s Garbage Collector manages memory in three generations: Generation 0, Generation 1, and Generation 2. There is no Generation 3.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a 'Managed Heap' in the context of .NET's Garbage Collector?

Options.OptionText: A stack segment reserved for method memory allocation.
A memory area used for storing thread metadata.
An area of memory where .NET manages the allocation and release of objects.
A storage unit for system configurations and settings.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Managed Heap is where .NET allocates and manages memory for objects. It is part of the automatic memory management system managed by the Garbage Collector.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following methods can be used to force immediate garbage collection in .NET?
m_Item1.Explanation: GC.Collect() is a method provided by .NET to manually trigger the garbage collection process. However, forcing GC is generally discouraged unless absolutely necessary.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to an object if no references point to it in a .NET application?

Options.OptionText: It becomes stored in the permanent area of memory.
It is moved to another generation for more frequent collection.
It becomes eligible for garbage collection.
It is immediately deleted from memory.

m_Item1.Explanation: An object becomes eligible for garbage collection when it is no longer referenced by any part of the application, allowing the GC to reclaim the memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about the 'Dispose' method is true in the context of .NET's Garbage Collection?

Options.OptionText: Dispose method handles the cleanup of managed resources automatically.
Dispose method is used to explicitly release unmanaged resources.
Dispose method is called by the Garbage Collector before reclaiming memory.
Dispose method should never be implemented by an application developer.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Dispose method is part of the IDisposable interface and is used to explicitly free unmanaged resources, such as file handles, database connections, etc.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In .NET, which of the following is primarily affected by increasing the frequency of garbage collection?

Options.OptionText: The application’s response time during execution.

m_Item1.Explanation: Frequent garbage collection can affect the application’s response time because the GC process pauses the execution to clean up unused objects, impacting performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of 'Finalizers' in .NET Garbage Collection?

Options.OptionText: To conduct memory allocation in managed code.
To handle the cleanup of managed resources accessible through interop.
To perform cleanup operations on unmanaged resources before an object is reclaimed by the Garbage Collector.
To execute asynchronous tasks related to system memory.

m_Item1.Explanation: Finalizers are used to clean up unmanaged resources just before an object is destroyed by the Garbage Collector, providing a way to ensure that unmanaged resources are properly released.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of creating separate AppDomains in a .NET application?

Options.OptionText: To isolate executed code, thereby increasing application robustness.
To improve the application's execution speed.
To allow garbage collection to run more efficiently.

m_Item1.Explanation: AppDomains provide an isolation boundary for executing managed code, which increases robustness by containing errors and security vulnerabilities within the isolated domain.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used to create a new AppDomain in a .NET application?
m_Item1.Explanation: The CreateDomain method of the AppDomain class is used to create a new application domain, allowing managed applications to run code isolated from the primary application.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can an assembly be loaded into a specific AppDomain?
m_Item1.Explanation: The AppDomain.Load() method is used to load an assembly into the specific application domain. It provides the flexibility to specify the assembly by name or by path.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason for unloading an AppDomain?

Options.OptionText: To reclaim memory utilized by the application domain.
To update the assembly versions dynamically.
To repair a corrupted state within the AppDomain.
To improve the speed of the garbage collector.

m_Item1.Explanation: Unloading an AppDomain does not directly affect the speed of the garbage collector. It is mainly unloaded to free up resources, update assemblies, or recover from errors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which event is triggered when an AppDomain is about to unload?
m_Item1.Explanation: The DomainUnload event is triggered when an AppDomain is about to be unloaded. It allows code to execute any cleanup operations that are necessary before the domain is removed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary role of the garbage collector in .NET memory management?

Options.OptionText: To manually free memory allocated by the programmer
To automatically manage memory allocation and deallocation
To handle pointer arithmetic for memory operations

m_Item1.Explanation: The garbage collector in .NET is responsible for automatically managing memory. It frees memory allocated to objects that are no longer in use by the application, thus avoiding memory leaks and eliminating the need for manual memory management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about the Stack and Heap in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Stack is used for dynamic memory allocation, while Heap is used for static memory allocation.
Both Stack and Heap can grow automatically as needed.
Stack is used for static memory allocation and simple variables, while Heap is used for dynamic memory allocation and objects.
Heap memory is faster to access compared to Stack memory.

m_Item1.Explanation: In .NET, the Stack is used for static memory allocation and holds simple variables and function call information, while the Heap is used for dynamic memory allocation to store objects and data structures that require a variable size.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which .NET garbage collection technique uses generations to improve application performance?
m_Item1.Explanation: Generational Garbage Collection is the technique used in .NET, which divides objects into generations based on their lifespan. This approach enhances performance by allowing the garbage collector to focus on collecting younger objects more frequently compared to older objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In .NET, which method can be utilized to forcefully trigger garbage collection?
m_Item1.Explanation: The GC.Collect() method in .NET can be used to forcefully initiate garbage collection. However, using it excessively is not recommended as it may lead to performance issues.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'using' statement in C# with regard to memory management?

Options.OptionText: To ensure that an IDisposable object releases its resources
To automatically manage the scope of a local variable

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'using' statement in C# is used to automatically dispose of an IDisposable object once it goes out of scope. This is particularly useful for releasing unmanaged resources like file handles or database connections.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about the Common Language Specification (CLS) in .NET is true?

Options.OptionText: CLS ensures that all .NET languages must use the same syntax.
CLS defines a set of rules for C++ programming in .NET.
CLS is a subset of the Common Type System (CTS) that ensures language interoperability.
CLS is responsible for memory management within the .NET runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: CLS is a set of rules and constraints to ensure interoperability among languages using .NET. It is a subset of the CTS.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of the Common Type System (CTS) in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To define how runtime garbage collection is performed.
To enable cross-language integration by defining language-neutral data types.
To specify the security model for .NET applications.
To compile code into an intermediate language (IL).

m_Item1.Explanation: CTS provides a standard that all languages must adhere to in terms of type definitions to ensure type safety and compatibility.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a goal of the Common Language Specification (CLS)?

Options.OptionText: To allow different .NET languages to interact with each other.
To define a common syntax for all .NET languages.
To provide a foundation for language interoperability.
To ensure consistent data types across .NET languages.

m_Item1.Explanation: CLS does not aim to define a common syntax for all .NET languages; it focuses on interoperability and language compatibility.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of .NET, which statement is true about the Common Type System (CTS)?

Options.OptionText: CTS ensures all .NET languages compile to machine code directly.
CTS defines cross-language interoperability and provides a library of pre-defined classes.
CTS provides the data types and their behavior as they are understood by the CLR.
CTS manages memory allocation for all objects in a .NET application.

m_Item1.Explanation: CTS specifies how types are defined and used in the runtime, facilitating communication between languages by using a common set of types.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the Common Language Specification (CLS) facilitate developers in .NET?

Options.OptionText: By ensuring that developers can write code exclusively in C#.
By providing a high-level syntax for language development.
By ensuring that code libraries can be consumed by any .NET-compliant language.
By defining optimized algorithms for language-specific implementations.

m_Item1.Explanation: CLS provides guidelines and rules to ensure that libraries and applications can be used across different .NET languages, promoting language interoperability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following classes in C# is used for encrypting data using the AES algorithm?
m_Item1.Explanation: The AesCryptoServiceProvider class provides the methods for encrypting and decrypting data using the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm. It is the recommended way to use AES encryption in .NET.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the SecureString class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: To securely connect to a database.
To store sensitive string data in memory with encryption.
To securely transmit data over network connections.

m_Item1.Explanation: The SecureString class is used to store sensitive information, such as passwords, in memory. It provides a way to keep the data encrypted while in use, reducing the risk of exposing string data in memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which namespace in .NET is primarily used for cryptographic services?
m_Item1.Explanation: The System.Security.Cryptography namespace provides cryptographic services, including secure encoding and decoding of data, as well as many other operations such as hashing and digital signing.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the RSACryptoServiceProvider class in .NET?

Options.OptionText: Generates random numbers for cryptographic operations.
Provides methods for asymmetric encryption and decryption using RSA.

m_Item1.Explanation: The RSACryptoServiceProvider class provides methods for asymmetric encryption and decryption using RSA. It supports key generation, encryption, decryption, and signing of data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In terms of .NET security, what is role-based security?

Options.OptionText: Assigning security roles to classes for encryption.
Determining access rights based on the user's role within an application.
Encrypting data with different roles of encryption keys.
Establishing secure network roles for data transmission.

m_Item1.Explanation: Role-based security in .NET is used to define and restrict what users can do based on their roles. It enables applications to authorize users based on their role memberships, which are often defined by the application's business logic.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of ASP.NET Core in .NET Core?

Options.OptionText: To provide a framework for building microservices

m_Item1.Explanation: ASP.NET Core is a cross-platform, high-performance framework for building modern, cloud-enabled, internet-connected web applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature of .NET Core makes it suitable for cloud deployments?

Options.OptionText: It is dependent on Windows APIs
Its tight integration with Microsoft Office

m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core's cross-platform nature and its modular architecture make it well-suited for cloud applications that require portability and scalability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an advantage of using Entity Framework Core in .NET Core applications?
m_Item1.Explanation: Entity Framework Core provides a powerful ORM framework that allows database-agnostic querying using LINQ, making it easy to work with various databases.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does .NET Core aid in developing microservices?

Options.OptionText: By limiting services to a single platform
By providing built-in support for large monolithic applications
By enabling small, independently deployable components
By enforcing the use of SOAP for all services

m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core's lightweight, modular architecture supports the development of microservices, allowing for small, independently deployable components.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to create a new project in .NET Core CLI?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'dotnet new' command is used to create a new project or file in .NET Core CLI, initializing the specified template.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the Mono project in .NET technology?

Options.OptionText: To transform C# code into a dynamic scripting language
To provide a multi-platform, open-source implementation of the .NET Framework
To improve the performance of Windows Forms applications
To facilitate the integration of XML and JSON web services

m_Item1.Explanation: The Mono project is primarily designed to offer a cross-platform, open-source implementation of the .NET Framework, allowing developers to run .NET applications on various operating systems like Linux and macOS, in addition to Windows.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a feature of the Mono runtime?

Options.OptionText: Direct support for iOS app development without Xamarin

m_Item1.Explanation: While Mono does support cross-platform .NET applications and integration with development environments like Visual Studio, direct support for iOS app development typically requires the use of Xamarin, which builds on Mono.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following operating systems can natively run applications developed using the Mono framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: Mono enables applications developed with .NET to run on various operating systems, including Windows, Linux, and macOS, making it a versatile choice for cross-platform development.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a feature of Xamarin in .NET technologies using C#?
m_Item1.Explanation: Xamarin provides access to platform-specific APIs, allowing developers to utilize native features of each platform. The statement 'No Access to Platform-Specific APIs' is incorrect.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does Xamarin.Forms primarily provide in the context of mobile development?

Options.OptionText: A platform to develop exclusively Android apps
A component for creating native UIs across iOS, Android, and Windows
A module for enhancing graphic rendering

m_Item1.Explanation: Xamarin.Forms allows developers to build user interfaces for iOS, Android, and Windows apps using a single, shared codebase, making it suitable for cross-platform UI development.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Xamarin, which tool is used to share C# code, tests, and business logic across multiple platforms?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Xamarin Portable Class Library (PCL) allows developers to write and share code across different .NET platforms while maintaining a single codebase for business logic and tests.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following frameworks is introduced with the promise of 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' for .NET developers?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Core was introduced to provide cross-platform support, allowing C# code to run on Windows, Linux, and macOS, fulfilling the promise of 'Write Once, Run Anywhere'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which version of .NET Framework introduced the concept of the Common Language Runtime (CLR)?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Common Language Runtime (CLR) was introduced in .NET Framework 1.0 as its core component, ensuring managed code execution across different language implementations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the key purpose of the .NET Standard?

Options.OptionText: To define a set of APIs for .NET implementations
To replace the Common Language Infrastructure
To support graphics rendering for games

m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Standard defines a set of APIs that all .NET platforms implement, providing developers with a uniform set of APIs across different .NET implementations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which .NET version offers minimal install and is designed to target small footprint devices?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET Micro Framework is specifically designed for resource-constrained devices, offering a minimal install with essential API support suitable for small footprint devices.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which version of .NET was announced as a unification of the .NET Framework, .NET Core, and Xamarin?
m_Item1.Explanation: .NET 5 was announced as the first version to unify and replace the various .NET platforms, bringing together .NET Framework, .NET Core, and Xamarin into a single platform.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of managed code in the .NET framework?

Options.OptionText: Automatic memory management via garbage collection
Faster execution compared to unmanaged code

m_Item1.Explanation: Managed code operates under the supervision of the .NET runtime (CLR) which provides services like automatic memory management via garbage collection, enhancing memory safety and aiding in faster development.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement about unmanaged code in the .NET framework is true?

Options.OptionText: It runs under the control of the CLR.
It does not require CLR for execution.
It can be written only in C#.

m_Item1.Explanation: Unmanaged code does not run under the control of the Common Language Runtime (CLR) and therefore does not benefit from features such as garbage collection or type safety.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which language feature is directly associated with managed code?
m_Item1.Explanation: Managed code benefits from the .NET framework's garbage collector, which automatically manages memory allocation and deallocation to prevent memory leaks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is required to execute unmanaged code within a managed application in .NET?
m_Item1.Explanation: To execute unmanaged code within a managed .NET application, you typically use Platform Invocation Services (P/Invoke) which allows managed code to call functions exported from unmanaged libraries.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following tools can be used to manage memory-related issues in unmanaged code?

Options.OptionText: Memory Leak Detection tool like Valgrind

m_Item1.Explanation: To deal with memory-related issues in unmanaged code, especially memory leaks, tools like Valgrind can be used as they are specifically designed for detecting and analyzing memory management problems in such environments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature in Visual Studio helps you to improve code quality by providing real-time validation and suggestions?
m_Item1.Explanation: Code Analysis in Visual Studio examines your code and provides suggestions for improving its quality and maintainability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Visual Studio, which window shows the list of variables and their current values while debugging?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Watch window in Visual Studio allows you to track the values of variables when you are debugging an application.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'Solution Explorer' in Visual Studio?
m_Item1.Explanation: Solution Explorer in Visual Studio is used for managing project files, references, and solutions structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using ILDASM (Intermediate Language Disassembler) in .NET technologies?

Options.OptionText: To convert a high-level language code into machine language code
To generate executable files from intermediate language code
To view and analyze the contents of an assembly in intermediate language
To optimize the runtime performance of an application

m_Item1.Explanation: ILDASM is primarily used to view and analyze the metadata and intermediate language (IL) code contained in a .NET assembly. It allows developers to inspect how their C# code is being translated into IL, but it does not convert high-level language to machine code or generate executables.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following outputs can you expect from using ILDASM on a .NET assembly?

Options.OptionText: The assembly source code in C#
The optimized machine code for the platform

m_Item1.Explanation: ILDASM provides a disassembled view of the Intermediate Language (IL) code along with the assembly's metadata. It does not provide output as C# source code, optimized machine code, or HTML documentation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using ILDASM, what would typically happen if you attempt to open an invalid .NET assembly file?

Options.OptionText: ILDASM will automatically repair the file and display its contents
ILDASM will display a message indicating that the file cannot be opened
ILDASM will crash and close immediately without any message
ILDASM will display a fabricated view of the assembly contents

m_Item1.Explanation: When ILDASM is used to open an invalid .NET assembly file, it will typically display an error message indicating that it cannot open or disassemble the file. It does not attempt to repair files or fabricate assembly contents, and it is designed to handle such errors gracefully without crashing.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used to read a single line of user input from the console in C#?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Console.ReadLine() method is used to read a single line of input from the console in a C# application.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default data type returned by Console.Read() method in C#?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Console.Read() method returns the ASCII value of the character as an integer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used to write formatted output to the console in C#?
m_Item1.Explanation:
In C#, both Console.Write() and Console.WriteLine() can be used to write output, but Console.WriteLine() is the commonly used method for formatted output that:

  • Writes text to the console

  • Supports formatting placeholders

  • Adds a newline at the end
